one_line_takeaway: Teaching a frontier LLM a random cipher in-context and fuzzing over many such ciphers yields a black-box, universal, scale-adaptive jailbreak (86.3% ASR on Claude 3.5 Sonnet HarmBench) that gets STRONGER as models get more capable, because complex encoding overloads the model's safety classification.
key_finding: Bijection learning achieves state-of-the-art ASR on all tested frontier models, beating the ensemble of 11 prior encoding attacks by at least 30 percentage points. Headline result: 86.3% ASR on Claude 3.5 Sonnet on the full HarmBench test set. The central scientific claim is a 'stronger-with-scale' phenomenon: within a model family, the more capable model has its peak-effectiveness bijection at both the highest MMLU score and the highest ASR, i.e. more capable models are more severely jailbroken. Dispersion has a smooth, non-monotonic effect (a middling range is strongest; too high and the model fails to learn the bijection, producing incoherent output; too low and it refuses). The paper advances a 'computational overload' hypothesis: bijection tasks consume the model's limited computation across simultaneous tasks (translate, answer, safety-classify), degrading safety classification. This is contrasted with the prior 'mismatched generalization' hypothesis. Defenses are weak: even a capability-matched Claude 3.5 Sonnet guardrail only cut ASR by ~10 points while tripling inference cost; weaker guards (Haiku) collapse into overrefusal (96% of benign intents triggered).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'bijection learning,' a black-box jailbreak attack that uses in-context learning to teach a frontier LLM an arbitrary, randomly-generated string-to-string encoding (a 'bijection language'), then passes the harmful query in that encoding to bypass safety mechanisms and decodes the reply. The paper's own claimed novelty (Section 1.3) is: (1) using random sampling to generate an effectively endless pool of encodings, enabling best-of-n 'prompt fuzzing' for a single harmful intent; (2) being the first to use quantitative hyperparameters (dispersion and encoding length) to smoothly scale encoding complexity, making the attack 'scale-adaptive' rather than relying on a model's memorized knowledge of one fixed cipher; and (3) demonstrating a Pareto frontier of model vulnerability where more capable models are MORE severely jailbroken. Critically, the paper distinguishes itself from cited encoding attacks (Yuan et al. Cipher, Wei et al., Jiang et al. ArtPrompt, Halawi et al. covert finetuning): those rely on a model's intrinsic/memorized knowledge of a specific encoding or require fine-tuning access, whereas this work teaches novel encodings purely in-context at inference time.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only query access to a deployed frontier LLM's text outputs (no tokenizer, gradient, or logit access) and no fine-tuning access. The attacker inserts a harmful intent into a fixed template consisting of a system message explaining the bijection, 10 in-context English-to-bijection teaching examples (Paul Graham essay excerpts), practice examples, and the encoded harmful query. The attack works even under temperature-0 sampling. The attacker can sample n random bijections (attack budget) and succeeds if any one yields a harmful response. The paper also models a defended target: an LLM wrapped by prompted input/output guard classifiers (Llama Guard-style).
limitations: Authors' stated limits: evaluations run on a limited number of closed-source models with large sweeps restricted to cheaper models due to cost; the attack is expensive, consuming ~25,000-50,000 input tokens per prompt (10 teaching examples), so it currently only works on large-context models. Unknown whether bijection jailbreaks can be done zero-shot / with fewer tokens on open-source models finetuned on bijection examples. Experiments give limited insight into non-English settings or white-box open-source attacks. Broader scale-adaptive claims rest solely on the single case study of bijection learning. Reviewer note: the 'stronger-with-scale' claim is drawn from a small number of model families and uses MMLU as the capability proxy; Gemini notably does NOT exhibit the two-stage scaling (ASR keeps improving with easier bijections, no turning point), a counterexample the paper acknowledges.

datasets_used: HarmBench test set (Mazeika et al. 2024, 320 intents)
HarmBench-35 (35 intents, 5 per risk category, used for parameter sweeps)
MMLU (Hendrycks et al. 2021, used under bijection encoding to measure capability degradation)
Custom set of 100 unambiguously benign intents (for guardrail overrefusal evaluation)
quantitative_results: Bijection learning ASR on HarmBench: Claude 3 Haiku 82.1%, Claude 3 Opus 78.1%, Claude 3.5 Sonnet 86.3%, GPT-4o-mini 64.1%, GPT-4o 59.1%
Bijection learning ASR on AdvBench-50: Claude 3 Haiku 92%, Claude 3 Opus 94%, Claude 3.5 Sonnet 94%, GPT-4o-mini 88%, GPT-4o 66%
Beats the 11-attack encoding ensemble by at least 30 percentage points across models
Best-of-n more than doubles single-attempt (best-of-1) ASR on all models
Optimal Sonnet config: 2-digit codomain, dispersion 16 (attack budget 20 on HarmBench, 9 on AdvBench-50)
Unguarded Sonnet-generation-only system baseline: 73.8% ASR on HarmBench
Sonnet guardrail (capability parity): reduces ASR by ~10 points, no benign refusals, but triples inference cost (3 Sonnet calls per query)
GPT-4o-mini guardrail: reduces ASR by 9 points but triggers on 12% of benign intents
Claude 3 Haiku guardrail: reduces ASR to near-zero but overrefuses on 96% of benign intents
Bijection prompt consumes ~25,000-50,000 input tokens (10 teaching examples)
baselines_compared: GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient, Zou et al. 2023) white-box suffix trained on Llama Guard 3 and transferred; HarmBench ASR 1.9%/0.6%/0.3%/13.1%/6.6% (Haiku/Opus/Sonnet/4o-mini/4o)
PAIR (Chao et al. 2023) black-box attacker-model refinement; HarmBench ASR 14.3%/13.8%/9.7%/22.5%/25%
TAP (Mehrotra et al. 2023) tree-of-attacks, fixed budget 58 (branch 3, width 5, depth 5); HarmBench ASR 18.1%/14.7%/10.3%/30.3%/34.4%

Ensemble of 11 prior encoding attacks (ASCII/ArtPrompt, Base64, leetspeak, ROT13, Morse, Caesar, Self-Cipher, keyboard, upside-down, word reversal, grid cipher), marked successful if any one succeeds; HarmBench ASR 39.7%/27.8%/20.9%/15.3%/28.1%
